1.matchText: the mainland prologue to that maritime story
1.blurb: Funan and the lower-Mekong trade world were the mainland prologue to the maritime empire of Srivijaya. The same monsoon-driven, layover-port economy that made Oc Eo rich on the Gulf of Thailand would, a few centuries later and out in the islands, build the Sumatran thalassocracy that taxed every ship between China and India.

Indianization is the Southeast Asian cousin of Sinicization: a great civilization radiating writing, religion, and statecraft across a wide region by prestige and trade rather than conquest. Korea absorbed Chinese script and Confucian administration the same way the Khmer absorbed Sanskrit and the devaraja — voluntarily, selectively, and without ever being invaded by the source.

1.matchText: the Sailendra dynasty associated with the Srivijayan world
1.blurb: The 'Java' that overshadowed eighth-century Water Chenla was most likely the Sailendra dynasty, the same Mahayana Buddhist power tied to the Srivijayan world that built Borobudur. Jayavarman II's whole founding ritual in Chapter 2 is staged as the throwing-off of exactly this overlordship.

Java is the maritime counterpart of the same voluntary Indianization. Majapahit Chapter 1 makes the identical 'no Indian army, prestige and trade, local elites pulling the culture toward themselves' argument for the island world, and contrasts it explicitly with Vietnam's conquered case.

2.blurb: The Khmer devaraja is one of history's clearest cases of divine kingship, and Old Kingdom Egypt is its most famous parallel: a pharaoh who was a living god, whose entire society's surplus was organized to build him a stone passage into the afterlife. Angkor Wat is, on the leading reading, the Khmer pyramid — a king's funerary machine for merging with a god.

The Maya k'uhul ajaw, the 'holy lord' who performed as a god on a pyramid stage, is the New World analogue of the devaraja. Both were divine kings whose legitimacy was literally built — temple-mountains as the throne of a sacred ruler — and in both civilizations the question of how much real power that sacred status carried is still debated.

Naram-Sin of Akkad put the horned crown of a god on his own head and wrote his name with the divine determinative — the Mesopotamian flirtation with god-kingship. Unlike the Khmer, Mesopotamia never made divine kingship a permanent six-century institution; it was the exception there and the rule at Angkor.

4.matchText: the contemporary Song dynasty in China
4.blurb: The Northern Song was Angkor's near-contemporary and its institutional opposite: a vast professional civil service recruited by competitive written examination, running the most bureaucratized state on Earth. Suryavarman I's answer to the same problem of binding officials was a mass loyalty oath carved into a doorway — sacred personal allegiance instead of meritocratic paperwork.

5.matchText: the great Gothic cathedrals of Europe
5.blurb: Angkor Wat and the great Gothic cathedrals of France were going up in the very same decades — Chartres and Notre-Dame de Paris begun in the 1160s, Angkor Wat finished by about 1150. Two civilizations at opposite ends of Eurasia, neither aware of the other, each pouring its whole surplus into the largest sacred stone structure it could imagine.

Hagia Sophia is the medieval world's boldest single roofed space; Angkor Wat is its largest integrated religious complex. The contrast is the point: Byzantine genius went up and inward into one impossible dome, Khmer genius went out and across into galleries, moats, and a walked cosmology.

Dai Viet — the emerging Vietnamese state Suryavarman II kept attacking — was the northern neighbor in the same crowded twelfth-century mainland that Champa and the Khmer fought across. The wars among Angkor, Champa, and Dai Viet are the mainland counterpart to the maritime rivalries that were reshaping the island world of Srivijaya at the same moment.

6.matchText: the seafaring island family from the Srivijaya story
6.blurb: The Cham spoke an Austronesian language — the seafaring island family of Malay, Javanese, and Polynesian from the Srivijaya story. Champa was, in effect, a maritime Austronesian power grafted onto the Indianized mainland, which is exactly why it could do what no land enemy could: come by sea and river and take Angkor from the lake side.

7.blurb: Avalokiteshvara, the compassion-bodhisattva whose face many scholars see on the Bayon, is a central figure of the Mahayana that crystallized in Gupta-era India. Jayavarman VII's bodhisattva-kingship is an Indian religious idea, carried east along the same Indianizing current as the Hindu gods, turned into a building program.

8.blurb: Zhou Daguan reached Angkor in 1296 as an envoy of Kublai Khan's Yuan — the same Mongol world, almost the same decade, that produced Marco Polo's account of China. Two outsiders' notebooks, written within years of each other, are what let us see the everyday texture of two great Asian civilizations that left little perishable record of their own.

Just as almost everything textured about the medieval Mongol world comes from Marco Polo and Ibn Battuta, almost everything textured about daily life at Angkor comes from Zhou Daguan. When a civilization leaves nothing perishable behind, the single surviving foreign traveler's report becomes the whole library.

8.matchText: having already hammered Champa and Dai Viet
8.blurb: Java is the fourth member of Kublai's southern demands, and the one with the strangest outcome. Majapahit Chapter 2: the 1293 Yuan expedition that Raden Wijaya turned into his own founding by alliance-then-ambush — the only one of the four defeated by a double-cross rather than tribute, terrain, or storm.

9.matchText: the new world of maritime commerce
9.blurb: The court's drift south toward Phnom Penh and the rivers tracked the same shift that had reshaped the island world: the center of Southeast Asian wealth was moving decisively to maritime trade. Inland temple-states like Angkor were on the wrong side of that shift; port polities, the heirs of the Srivijaya model, were on the right side of it.

10.blurb: Angkor and the Classic Maya are the two great test cases in the global debate over why complex societies unwind, and both have taught the same lesson: 'collapse' is almost always too simple a word. In both, the dramatic single-cause story (overpopulation, a drought, infrastructure failure) is the contested one, and the honest answer is a slow convergence of climate, religion, politics, and economics.

10.matchText: Tang Chang'an with its million people
10.blurb: Tang Chang'an, with perhaps a million people behind its walls, is the usual benchmark for the largest pre-industrial cities. The LiDAR surveys put Angkor in that conversation by sheer inhabited extent — not a denser city than Chang'an, but a far more spread-out one, plausibly the largest-footprint settlement of the pre-modern world.

10.matchText: the booming maritime trade of the era
10.blurb: Majapahit Chapter 7 is that booming maritime trade world in its 14th-century prime — the rice-and-spice thalassocracy the Khmer court was moving toward as Angkor's god-king economy wound down. The two narratives are the riverine and the maritime ends of the same regional shift.
